phrase: Analysis of the implant's behaviour revealed that it loaded a 64-bit payload DLL directly into the address space of explorer.exe using the LoadLibrary API
The threat actor staged a malicious DLL in a world-writable directory before forcing its loading into a legitimate browser process via a remote thread
Telemetry captured CreateRemoteThread calls preceded by WriteProcessMemory writing a DLL path into the memory of winlogon.exe
Post-exploitation activity included classic LoadLibrary-based DLL injection into the Windows print spooler process to host the implant
The backdoor achieved code execution within lsass.exe by planting a payload DLL and triggering its load via a remotely created thread
A kernel driver obtained via a BYOVD technique was used to map an unsigned payload DLL into a PPL-protected process
A trojanised version of a legitimate DLL was placed in the application directory to be side-loaded when the signed host binary launched
The loader persisted its core library by encoding it in a Registry value and reconstructing it into a host process's address space at runtime
On macOS endpoints, the threat group weaponised dylib hijacking to force a trusted Gatekeeper-approved application into loading a rogue shared library
Memory forensics revealed module stomping where the .text section of a legitimately loaded DLL was overwritten in-place with attacker shellcode
Mach-O injection was identified on compromised macOS hosts, with a rogue dylib loaded into a notarised application to evade detection
The group abused a vulnerable third-party driver to map an unsigned DLL directly into a protected system process, bypassing standard code integrity checks
A reflectively loaded PE image was identified mapped into the working set of svchost.exe with no corresponding file artefact on disk
The stager bootstrapped the second-stage implant entirely in memory using a modified ReflectiveDLLInjection loader to avoid touching the file system
Endpoint telemetry captured an unsigned PE being manually mapped into explorer.exe's address space using NtCreateSection and NtMapViewOfSection
The implant used a custom reflective loader stub that resolved all required exports at runtime, leaving no import table on disk to scan
Manual PE loading via NtCreateSection was used to bypass API-level hooks applied by the resident EDR product on LoadLibrary
The dropper used manual PE mapping to reconstruct the payload's import table and relocations in memory, avoiding the Windows loader entirely
The RAT loaded its plugin modules by reflectively mapping DLLs into a long-running trusted system process, making them invisible to module enumeration
Shellcode was written into a heap allocation within the victim process and execution was triggered by queuing a user-mode APC via NtQueueApcThread
The operator queued an asynchronous procedure call pointing to the implant shellcode within a suspended thread inside svchost.exe
An early-bird APC injection pattern was confirmed — shellcode was enqueued before the main thread of the spawned process began executing
VirtualAllocEx, WriteProcessMemory, and NtQueueApcThread were observed in sequence, indicating classic APC-based shellcode injection
The loader planted shellcode into a suspended process's thread queue before the process was resumed, a technique commonly known as early-bird injection
A cross-process section created with NtCreateSection was mapped into the victim process with NtMapViewOfSection to deliver the shellcode payload
The implant wrote shellcode into the target process heap and corrupted a SEH record to redirect execution — a variant of exception-handler hijacking
A Section object backed by the attacker's payload was projected into the victim's address space, avoiding the need for WriteProcessMemory
The Mockingjay technique was observed, with the attacker placing shellcode into an existing RWX memory region within a legitimate process
NtMapViewOfSection was used to share a physical memory page between the loader process and target process as a stealthy payload delivery channel
Memory analysis confirmed shellcode delivery via a remote APC queue, with the payload decrypted in-place immediately after the thread resumed
The threat actor staged shellcode inside a shared section object and projected it into a remote process to avoid detectable cross-process writes
APC injection via NtQueueApcThreadEx2 was used to queue execution of shellcode in an alertable thread inside a Windows system process
The dropper created a suspended instance of svchost.exe, unmapped its original image using NtUnmapViewOfSection, and mapped the implant in its place
Process hollowing was confirmed by comparing the on-disk and in-memory images of a running werfault.exe process — the PE headers did not match
RunPE-style hollowing was employed, with the attacker zeroing the original PE's memory and writing the decrypted payload before resuming the main thread
ZwUnmapViewOfSection followed by VirtualAllocEx was observed during process creation of cmd.exe, consistent with a process hollowing sequence
The Process Ghosting technique was identified — the payload executable was deleted mid-transaction before the process image could be mapped from it
A PPID-spoofed child process was created in a suspended state, hollowed, and loaded with the Cobalt Strike beacon before the thread was resumed
The stager performed hollow-process injection into a signed Windows binary, replacing its executable content with the RAT payload in memory
The actor patched the original entry point of a hollowed process to redirect execution to the implant shellcode upon thread resumption
Memory acquisition from the affected host revealed a mismatch between the on-disk PE and the in-memory image of a running system binary, indicating hollowing
Process herpaderping was used — the implant file was overwritten with innocuous content after being mapped into a child process, defeating file-based scanning
The implant abused the Windows thread pool by registering a malicious work callback via TpAllocWork that executed the shellcode payload on a worker thread
A pool party injection variant was identified in which the attacker queued shellcode execution through an I/O completion port worker thread
Execution of the injected payload was triggered via CreateThreadpoolWork, allowing the shellcode to run on a legitimate thread pool thread within the victim process
The threat actor avoided spawning new threads by scheduling shellcode through the Windows thread pool using TpPostWork, evading new-thread telemetry
A timer callback registered via CreateTimerQueueTimer was used to periodically invoke attacker-controlled shellcode within the targeted process
The loader hijacked an existing thread pool work item by overwriting its callback pointer, redirecting execution to the injected payload
Thread pool injection via TpAllocWork was used by the implant to execute code within svchost.exe without triggering remote-thread creation alerts
An alertable wait state was exploited — the actor queued an APC-based callback into a thread pool worker to execute shellcode inside the victim process
CreateRemoteThread was called against a long-running Chrome renderer process to host the credential harvesting module without spawning a new visible process
Memory forensics on the compromised workstation identified an unsigned thread in explorer.exe whose start address pointed to an unbacked memory region
The RAT used OpenProcess with PROCESS_ALL_ACCESS, followed by CreateRemoteThread, to initiate shellcode execution within the targeted system process
NtCreateThreadEx was used in place of CreateRemoteThread to inject a thread into a SYSTEM-level process while bypassing some security product hooks
RtlCreateUserThread was invoked to inject execution into a protected process that prevented the use of the standard CreateRemoteThread API
A remote thread was created inside an already-running lsass.exe process to execute a credential harvesting shellcode payload with SYSTEM privileges
An unsigned thread of execution was identified in spoolsv.exe originating from a separate process context, consistent with remote thread-based injection
The actor suspended a thread within winlogon.exe, modified its CONTEXT record to redirect RIP to the shellcode, and resumed it to trigger execution
SetThreadContext was called after SuspendThread to overwrite the instruction pointer of a remote thread, redirecting execution to attacker-controlled code
NtSetContextThread was used to alter the execution context of a suspended thread in lsass.exe, pointing it to injected shellcode in an adjacent allocation
The threat actor hijacked an alertable thread by modifying its CONTEXT.Rip field before calling NtAlertResumeThread to resume execution at the shellcode
All threads within the target process were suspended simultaneously; code was patched inline before they were resumed — a form of thread context manipulation
Memory analysis revealed that a thread's instruction pointer had been modified to point outside any mapped module, indicating thread context hijacking
The loader located a waiting thread inside svchost.exe, suspended it via NtSuspendThread, and overwrote its program counter with the payload entry point
The Atom Bombing technique was observed, with the actor writing shellcode into the global atom table and triggering its execution via a callback in the target process
A variant of atom bombing was identified in which global atom table entries were leveraged to deliver shellcode into a target process without standard injection APIs
PROPagate injection was used by the implant, abusing SetProp window property callbacks to execute attacker-controlled code inside a window-owning process
The Windows Notification Facility was weaponised to queue a kernel-mode callback that triggered shellcode execution in the context of the victim user-mode process
A complete PE image with a valid MZ/PE header was mapped into the address space of spoolsv.exe and executed by calling its entry point as a thread start routine
The loader reconstructed the payload's import table and applied base relocations before transferring execution to the injected PE's entry point within the host process
Forensic analysis identified a fully formed PE image in the memory of a running system process with no corresponding executable file on the file system
Process herpaderping was used to overwrite the loader file with a benign binary immediately after mapping, leaving scanning tools with an innocuous on-disk file
WerFault.exe was spawned as a host for PE injection — the implant wrote a complete executable into its address space before calling its entry point
A gargoyle-style memory technique was employed, alternating the injected PE's memory between non-executable and executable states to evade periodic memory scans
The implant staged payload inside a sacrificial process, then duplicated the section into lsass.exe, projecting a full PE image without using WriteProcessMemory
A vulnerable signed driver was exploited using BYOVD to map payload pages formatted as a PE image directly into a protected Windows system process
The implant injected a complete DLL image into a remote process by manually resolving its import table and applying relocations before redirecting a thread to the entry point
Process doppelgänging was identified — the attacker created a transacted file write of the payload, mapped it into a new process, then rolled back the transaction to remove file evidence
The implant exploited NTFS transactions to overwrite a legitimate binary's mapped image in memory, a technique known as process doppelgänging
Investigators confirmed a doppelgänging variant in which the actor substituted the in-memory process image via a rolled-back NTFS transaction before resuming execution
On compromised Linux endpoints, the threat actor set the LD_PRELOAD environment variable to force every spawned child process to load a malicious shared object
The implant used ptrace system calls to attach to a running process, inject shellcode into its address space, and redirect its instruction pointer to the payload
VDSO hijacking was observed on Linux hosts, with the actor overwriting the kernel-provided virtual DSO mapping to redirect userland system call dispatching
Fork-and-exec injection was used on Linux — the implant forked, injected a shared library into the child via ptrace, then exec'd the legitimate binary to disguise the process
The stealer registered a malicious in-process COM server under HKCU\Software\Classes\CLSID to intercept COM object instantiation by a targeted legitimate application
DCOM activation was abused to force the COM surrogate process (dllhost.exe) to load and execute the attacker's payload DLL as part of object instantiation
A custom application compatibility shim database was installed to redirect code execution within the target application to attacker-controlled code at runtime
COM object hijacking was leveraged by registering a rogue InprocServer32 entry under the current user's registry hive, intercepting COM loading by a privileged process
The actor executed a base64-encoded PowerShell command via a spawned child process to download and invoke the second-stage implant entirely from memory
Endpoint telemetry captured powershell.exe launched with the -EncodedCommand and -NonInteractive flags to bypass transcript logging and AMSI inspection
A heavily obfuscated PowerShell one-liner using nested Invoke-Expression calls was used to fetch and execute the stager payload from an actor-controlled C2
The threat group abused PowerShell remoting over WinRM to execute commands laterally across multiple domain-joined hosts without deploying additional tooling
A malicious Office macro spawned a PowerShell process with a download cradle that retrieved and reflectively loaded the final implant directly into memory
An IEX download cradle constructed from concatenated string fragments was used in PowerShell to evade static signature matching during payload retrieval
A batch file dropped during the initial access phase was used to enumerate local accounts, disable Windows Defender, and establish a new backdoor administrator account
Post-compromise activity included extensive use of cmd.exe to chain reconnaissance commands — including ipconfig, net user, and tasklist — immediately after gaining access
The actor used cmd /c with pipe-chained commands to collect system information and write the output to a staging file for later exfiltration
A macro embedded in a weaponised Word document written in VBA downloaded and executed the first-stage dropper on the victim machine immediately upon document open
A VBScript file was dropped to the Windows Temp directory and registered as a scheduled task to re-execute the implant daily, providing redundant persistence
FIN7-affiliated actors deployed a Python-based backdoor disguised as a point-of-sale diagnostic utility to avoid triggering AV signatures tuned for native executables
A malicious .js file delivered via a phishing email was executed by wscript.exe and used as the first stage, downloading and launching the primary payload
The actor leveraged a Node.js-based implant to achieve cross-platform post-exploitation capability without relying on compiled native binaries
Mimikatz was executed with the sekurlsa::logonpasswords module to extract plaintext credentials and NTLM hashes from LSASS process memory
The actor used the MiniDump export of comsvcs.dll via rundll32 to produce a memory dump of lsass.exe, avoiding deployment of Mimikatz on disk
ProcDump was transferred to the compromised host and used to create a full minidump of lsass.exe, which was subsequently exfiltrated for offline credential extraction
Memory forensics identified an unsigned process opening lsass.exe with PROCESS_VM_READ access — a strong indicator of credential material harvesting
The threat actor used Nanodump to create an LSASS dump with a deliberately malformed minidump signature to bypass AV detections targeting standard dump formats
The built-in Task Manager was used on a compromised host to create a memory dump of lsass.exe — a low-noise, living-off-the-land credential dumping approach
The reg save command was used against the HKLM\SAM and HKLM\SYSTEM hives to extract a copy of the local SAM database for offline password cracking
The threat operator exported the SAM and SYSTEM hives using reg.exe, transferred them to an attacker-controlled server, and cracked NTLM hashes with hashcat
Volume Shadow Copy access was leveraged to extract the SAM, SYSTEM, and SECURITY hive files from a shadow snapshot without locking the live registry
LSA secrets were extracted from the SECURITY hive to recover cached domain credentials and service account passwords stored by the Windows LSA subsystem
Mimikatz lsadump::secrets was executed on the compromised host to dump LSA secret values including service account plaintext credentials and machine account hashes
DCSync was performed using Mimikatz's lsadump::dcsync module, impersonating a domain controller to pull NTLM hashes for all privileged accounts without touching LSASS
The actor used Impacket's secretsdump.py to perform a DCSync attack against the domain controller and extract the NTLM hash for the krbtgt account
Network traffic analysis captured MS-DRSR replication requests originating from a workstation — a strong indicator of a DCSync credential extraction operation
FIN7 delivered a weaponised Word document via targeted spearphishing emails, with the embedded macro downloading a Carbanak implant on execution
Initial access was attributed to a phishing campaign distributing password-protected Excel files containing XLM 4.0 macros that executed the dropper on open
A ZIP attachment containing a digitally signed LNK file was used to evade mail gateway inspection — double-clicking the shortcut silently executed the stager
The phishing email delivered an ISO image containing a hidden PE and a visible decoy document; mounting the ISO and opening the decoy triggered execution
An HTML smuggling attachment was used to reconstruct and drop an encoded payload on the victim's local file system, bypassing email gateway attachment scanning
A PDF attachment was weaponised to present a fake Adobe Reader update prompt; clicking the link initiated a drive-by download of the first-stage implant
A targeted spearphishing email contained a link to an actor-controlled site that harvested Office 365 credentials via a convincing Microsoft login page clone
APT29 delivered spearphishing links via Microsoft Teams messages, impersonating IT support personnel to direct victims to a credential-harvesting landing page
The group used Evilginx2 to perform adversary-in-the-middle phishing, capturing both credentials and session cookies to bypass MFA on targeted accounts
OAuth consent phishing links were distributed that, when approved by the victim, granted the actor persistent access to the victim's mailbox and OneDrive
Victims were directed to a cloned SharePoint portal hosted on a typosquatted domain to harvest Microsoft 365 credentials with convincing UX
The actor sent targeted messages via LinkedIn, posing as a recruiter, with links to fake job offer documents that delivered the initial dropper on download
A WhatsApp-based spearphishing campaign delivered malicious links to employees of targeted financial institutions, redirecting them to credential-harvesting sites
Automated password guessing was observed against the externally exposed SSH management interface using a curated list of commonly used and default credentials
Brute force authentication attempts against the organisation's public-facing RDP service were identified in firewall logs, using a tool consistent with Hydra
The threat actor systematically submitted credential pairs to the web application login page at a rate designed to avoid account lockout thresholds
Captured NTLMv2 challenge-response hashes from a rogue SMB server were cracked offline using hashcat with a custom rule set and a compromised wordlist
The actor cracked captured Kerberos AS-REP hashes for accounts with pre-authentication disabled using GPU-accelerated offline cracking with hashcat
NTLM hashes extracted from the SAM database were subjected to offline cracking using a hybrid attack combining dictionary words with common corporate password patterns
A low-and-slow password spray was conducted against the organisation's Okta tenant, cycling through a list of target accounts with a single common password per wave
Authentication logs for the Microsoft 365 tenant showed distributed password spray activity originating from multiple residential IPs at a rate below lockout policy thresholds
The threat actor used MSOLSpray to conduct password spraying against Azure AD-backed authentication endpoints, avoiding detection by spacing requests over several hours
A password spray against the Citrix gateway was identified in which a single commonly used corporate password was tested against all enumerated employee accounts
Credential stuffing was performed against the customer portal using credential pairs sourced from a dark web breach compilation containing millions of records
Automated login attempts leveraging previously breached email and password combinations were identified targeting the organisation's VPN authentication endpoint
The actor deployed a credential stuffing tool that replayed leaked credentials from multiple prior breaches against the corporate web application login page
Web application firewall logs revealed high-volume credential stuffing traffic, with each attempt originating from a different IP address in a rotating residential proxy pool
Lateral movement was conducted via Remote Desktop Protocol using domain credentials harvested during the initial access phase, with connections observed to multiple internal servers
The actor enabled RDP on compromised hosts by modifying the fDenyTSConnections registry value and subsequently used it to spread to additional systems
Network traffic analysis captured RDP sessions originating from the initial beachhead host to three internal domain controllers over a 48-hour period
The threat actor tunnelled RDP traffic over an established SSH reverse tunnel to a compromised Linux server, bypassing network egress filtering
SharpRDP was used to execute commands on remote hosts over RDP without requiring a graphical session, reducing the operator's exposure footprint
The actor used PSExec to copy the implant to the ADMIN$ share and execute it as a service on remote hosts during the lateral movement phase
CrackMapExec was used to authenticate to discovered hosts via SMB using harvested credentials and execute payloads via the C$ administrative share
Lateral movement artefacts included the implant binary present in the Windows\Temp directory of multiple hosts, deposited via SMB administrative share access
Impacket's smbexec.py was used to execute commands on remote hosts without dropping a service binary, relying instead on named pipe communication
The actor pivoted to cloud-hosted Linux instances by authenticating via SSH using RSA private keys extracted from the initially compromised developer workstation
SSH-based lateral movement was identified on Linux endpoints, with the actor using harvested authorised_keys entries to authenticate without a password
WinRM over HTTPS was used as a lateral movement channel to execute PowerShell commands on internal servers without generating SMB-based detection artefacts
Evil-WinRM was used by the threat actor to establish an interactive remote management session on a targeted server using domain administrator credentials
The backdoor established persistence by writing a value pointing to the implant binary under HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run
A registry Run key entry was added under HKLM\CurrentVersion\Run with a name resembling a legitimate Windows component to avoid casual inspection
The implant used reg.exe to create a Run key value that ensured execution at every user logon, surviving reboots and antivirus remediation attempts
Persistence was achieved via a RunOnce key that re-dropped and relaunched the implant on the next boot cycle if the primary Run key was removed
The actor added the implant's path to the HKLM Run key using a PowerShell one-liner executed in the context of a SYSTEM-level process
A WMI event subscription was registered to execute the second-stage stager at system startup as an alternative persistence mechanism
The threat actor modified the HKCU Run registry value to point to a renamed copy of the implant placed in the %APPDATA%\Roaming directory
The actor modified the Winlogon Userinit registry value to append the implant's path, ensuring execution during every interactive logon sequence
A malicious DLL was registered under the Winlogon Notify registry subkey to achieve persistent execution triggered at every Windows logon event
The implant installed itself as a Winlogon helper DLL, causing it to execute in session zero context before the user's desktop was fully initialised
Legitimate desktop LNK files were modified to prepend the implant path, causing it to execute silently alongside the intended application on each launch
A malicious shortcut was placed in the user's Startup folder, configured to launch the backdoor on every logon with a minimised window to avoid detection
Widely used application shortcuts in the Start Menu were backdoored to point to a wrapper script that launched both the implant and the legitimate application
A scheduled task was registered via schtasks.exe to execute the payload binary every 15 minutes under the SYSTEM account, providing privileged persistent execution
The threat actor used the ITaskService COM interface to create a scheduled task programmatically, leaving no schtasks.exe process creation event in the logs
A hidden scheduled task was identified within the Microsoft\Windows\SideShow task path, disguised among legitimate tasks to avoid manual inspection
The implant modified an existing legitimate Windows maintenance task to append a malicious action step pointing to the payload executable
The actor created a scheduled task named 'Windows Update Helper' configured to run at user logon with highest available privileges
Analysis of the compromised host's task scheduler revealed a task registered with XML-based configuration that bypassed standard schtasks.exe telemetry
A scheduled task was used to re-deploy the implant from a hardcoded URL if the main executable was deleted, providing resilient persistence
The threat actor exported and re-imported a modified task XML definition using schtasks /create /xml to register the persistence mechanism without invoking common command-line patterns
A cron entry was added under /etc/cron.d on the compromised Linux server to re-establish the reverse shell every 30 minutes as root
The actor modified the root crontab to include an entry that executed the implant script at reboot, providing persistent access following system restarts
Forensic examination of the compromised Linux host revealed a cron entry that downloaded and executed a shell script from an actor-controlled server on an hourly basis
The at command was used to schedule remote execution of the dropper on a target host during lateral movement, avoiding the creation of a persistent scheduled task
at.exe was observed scheduling the payload to execute 10 minutes after the command was issued, a technique used to delay execution and complicate forensic attribution
The implant modified the Windows Defender policy registry key to disable real-time monitoring before deploying its payload to avoid immediate detection
The threat actor stopped the Windows Defender service and removed its definition files using PowerShell, leaving the host unprotected for subsequent payload deployment
All running processes associated with the installed EDR agent were killed using taskkill.exe before the actor proceeded with credential dumping
A WDAC bypass technique was applied to prevent the EDR product's driver from loading at the next boot cycle, effectively blinding host-based monitoring
The threat actor invoked the endpoint protection product's own administrative uninstaller using the correct management password, cleanly removing it
The implant's installation directory was added to the Windows Defender exclusion list via PowerShell Set-MpPreference to prevent subsequent scanning of its files
Before initiating lateral movement, the actor terminated all security product agent processes identified by a hardcoded list of known EDR and AV process names
A GPO modification was used to disable Windows security event logging across all domain-joined endpoints before the actor began their destructive actions
The actor invoked wevtutil.exe to clear the Security, System, and Application event logs on the compromised host immediately following privilege escalation
The auditpol.exe utility was used to set all audit subcategories to No Auditing, preventing the endpoint from generating security-relevant event log entries
netsh advfirewall set allprofiles state off was executed on compromised hosts to disable host-based firewall rules and permit inbound C2 traffic
The threat actor added a Windows Firewall inbound rule permitting traffic to a high-numbered port used by the implant's listener to prevent connection blocking
Host-based firewall rules were modified via netsh to permit outbound communication on the non-standard port used for command-and-control beaconing
The actor gained access to a network management appliance by authenticating with the factory-default administrator credentials that had never been changed
Initial access to the cloud infrastructure management console was obtained using default vendor credentials present on an internet-exposed administrative interface
The threat actor authenticated to the domain controller using a compromised domain administrator account obtained via credential dumping from an earlier compromised host
A domain service account with domain-wide read access was abused for lateral movement, authenticating to file servers and SQL instances across the network
A developer account with workstation local administrator rights was leveraged for lateral movement across the development environment without triggering privileged account alerts
The actor used a legitimate help desk domain account — compromised weeks prior — to access the ticketing system and escalate privileges using a social engineering pretext
A shared local administrator account with an identical password across all workstations was used by the actor for lateral movement via SMB after credential extraction
The built-in local Administrator account, re-enabled via net user, was used to authenticate to systems where domain accounts lacked local admin rights
A backdoor local account created during an earlier intrusion phase was used weeks later to re-establish persistent access after the initial implant was remediated
Azure AD credentials stolen via an adversary-in-the-middle phishing kit were used to authenticate to Microsoft 365 services and access sensitive SharePoint content
The actor abused a misconfigured Azure managed identity attached to a compute instance to access storage accounts and retrieve secrets from Key Vault
OAuth access tokens intercepted via session hijacking were replayed against the Microsoft Graph API to read mail, contacts, and OneDrive files
A compromised AWS IAM access key with AdministratorAccess permissions was used to enumerate S3 buckets and exfiltrate sensitive configuration data
The threat actor created a local administrator account using net user /add and immediately added it to the local Administrators group via net localgroup
A backdoor local user account named 'svc_backup' was created to blend with legitimate service accounts and avoid detection in user enumeration output
PowerShell's New-LocalUser and Add-LocalGroupMember cmdlets were used to create a privileged backdoor account with administrative rights
A hidden local account with a trailing dollar sign was created to suppress it from the default net user listing on the compromised workstation
The actor created a local account and added it to the Remote Desktop Users group to enable persistent RDP access without domain credential exposure
A new local administrator account was created using the computer management console (compmgmt.msc) to avoid generating schtasks or net.exe command-line artefacts
A local account mirroring the naming convention of existing IT service accounts was provisioned on multiple hosts as a redundant persistence mechanism
A new domain user account was created via LDAP and immediately added to the Domain Admins group, providing the actor with persistent privileged domain access
The threat actor provisioned a rogue Active Directory user object with a name resembling a decommissioned employee account to avoid scrutiny
The attacker used Impacket's addcomputer.py to provision a machine account in the domain, which was subsequently used as a Kerberos principal for delegated access
A new AWS IAM user with programmatic access and AdministratorAccess policy attached was created as a persistent backdoor inside the compromised AWS organisation
The actor provisioned a rogue Azure AD guest user account and assigned it Contributor-level permissions on a subscription containing sensitive workloads
A Google Cloud service account with owner-level IAM permissions was created in a non-production project as an out-of-band persistent access mechanism
The implant was protected with a custom packer that generated a unique binary hash on each compilation run, defeating reputation-based and hash-based detection
A commercial packer was applied to the dropper executable to encrypt its payload section and produce a stub that decrypted and loaded the real code at runtime
The actor used a runtime packer that decrypted and loaded the PE payload directly into memory, leaving security tools with only the encrypted stub on disk
C source code for the implant was delivered to the victim host and compiled on-system using an already-installed GCC toolchain to bypass binary-scanning controls
The actor transferred fragmented source code to the victim host and assembled and compiled it locally using MSBuild, avoiding delivery of a precompiled executable
PowerShell commands were split across multiple concatenated string variables and joined at runtime to defeat string-matching based command-line detection rules
Environment variable substitution was used to construct cmd.exe commands character by character, producing obfuscated command lines that evaded script-block logging patterns
The actor used certutil -decode to reconstruct a base64-encoded payload from a text file, avoiding direct download of a PE and leveraging a trusted system binary
The payload was delivered as a multi-layered obfuscated PowerShell script that peeled each encoding layer using Invoke-Expression to reveal and execute the next stage
The final-stage implant was AES-256 encrypted with a key derived from a unique hardware identifier on the victim host, preventing offline analysis of the sample
The actor concealed the implant configuration data within the EXIF metadata of a JPEG file uploaded to a legitimate image hosting service
The dropper embedded its second-stage payload as a base64-encoded string appended to a JPEG comment field, masquerading as a legitimate image file
XOR-encoded configuration strings were embedded within the implant's binary and decoded at runtime using a single-byte key derived from the process start time
The Cobalt Strike beacon was configured with a malleable C2 profile that mimicked legitimate jQuery download traffic over HTTPS, blending into normal web traffic
Command-and-control communications were conducted exclusively over HTTPS using a TLS certificate issued for a domain typosquatting a legitimate cloud provider
The implant transmitted collected data to its C2 via HTTP POST requests with payloads structured to resemble standard web analytics beacon traffic
Beacon traffic was routed through a compromised legitimate e-commerce site acting as a domain-fronted reverse proxy to the actual C2 infrastructure
The Brute Ratel C4 framework was deployed with an HTTP profile mimicking Microsoft Graph API responses to blend C2 traffic with legitimate Microsoft 365 activity
The implant exfiltrated collected data to an actor-controlled FTP server by establishing outbound connections over port 21, which was permitted by the firewall policy
SFTP was used to exfiltrate staged archives to an actor-controlled server, blending with legitimate administrative file transfer activity observed in network baselines
The implant received tasking by polling a compromised email inbox via IMAP, reading commands embedded in the body of specially crafted message subjects
Collected data was exfiltrated via SMTP by encoding it into multi-part email attachments sent to an actor-controlled inbox, using a legitimate mail relay as a relay hop
The Sliver implant was configured to use DNS over HTTPS for command-and-control, tunnelling all tasking through encrypted DNS queries to a controlled resolver
C2 communication was conducted by encoding commands into DNS TXT record query names directed at an actor-controlled authoritative name server
Dnscat2 was deployed to tunnel a full interactive shell session over DNS, with all traffic appearing as standard recursive DNS queries to perimeter monitoring tools
The implant used a DNS-based dead drop resolver, querying a TXT record on a legitimate public DNS provider to retrieve the current C2 IP address
Rubeus was used to request Kerberos TGS tickets for all identified SPNs, with the encrypted tickets written to disk for subsequent offline cracking
The actor authenticated to an additional internal subnet via RDP using credentials recovered from a browser credential store on the first compromised endpoint
A PowerShell script leveraging the Microsoft Graph SDK was used to enumerate and export mailbox content from compromised Exchange Online accounts
